accepted_017.assessment:
This is a timely and thought-provoking paper that leverages a rare longitudinal cortical dataset to argue for continued, task-relevant representational learning after behavioral mastery. The geometric perspective via max-margin growth, the synthetic modeling with a targeted ablation, and the simple linear-theory explanation of the overtraining reversal effect make for a coherent and cross-disciplinary contribution. However, the neural reanalysis would benefit from stronger methodological detail and controls—particularly around scaling/normalization for margin comparability, movement/state confounds, cross-day generalization, and more comprehensive statistics—before the central claims can be considered fully established. I view the work as a promising step that crystallizes important hypotheses and offers concrete predictions; with added rigor and clarity on the neural analyses, it could merit acceptance. In its current form, I lean toward a weak accept, contingent on addressing the methodological gaps and presenting key statistical controls in the main text.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_047.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and practical problem: producing calibrated epistemic uncertainty for fine-tuned implicit functions under sparse and shifted inputs. The proposed Dropsembles + EWC pipeline is simple, effective, and well-motivated for decoder-focused uncertainty, with convincing demonstrations across synthetic and real settings and a thoughtful medical application. The main technical contribution is incremental—turning dropout-trained weights into independent subnetworks for fine-tuning and ensembling, with EWC to curb drift—but it is a useful, domain-tailored engineering solution that appears to outperform MC dropout and approach deep-ensemble quality at substantially reduced pretraining cost. The work would be strengthened by deeper comparisons to alternative efficient UQ baselines (SWA/SWAG, Laplace/KFAC-Laplace, snapshot/fast geometric ensembles, DPS), clearer EWC implementation details under masking, and more extensive evaluation on real clinical cases. Overall, I view this as a solid, practically impactful contribution with moderate novelty and good empirical support; I lean toward borderline accept, contingent on addressing the methodological clarifications and augmenting comparisons.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_053.assessment:
This paper delivers a timely, high-impact, and carefully executed multimodal audit that substantially advances our empirical understanding of AI training data provenance, use restrictions, and representation. Its main contributions—cross-modal scope, explicit tracing of upstream source terms (including synthetic model terms), and longitudinal representation analyses—are novel and significant for both researchers and practitioners. While methodological details around annotation reliability, deduplication, and statistical inference should be strengthened, the core findings are well supported and align with emerging evidence from complementary studies on transparency, licensing, and supply-chain compliance. The release of the underlying audit and tools will provide immediate utility for dataset selection, compliance screening, and policy discourse. I believe this work is well-suited for ICLR’s Datasets and Benchmarks track and recommend acceptance after addressing the noted clarifications and adding sensitivity analyses that bolster the robustness of the headline claims.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_059.assessment:
This paper makes a compelling case—both conceptually and empirically—that endowing 2D backbones with 3D spatial awareness via differentiable rendering substantially improves embodied learning. The methodology is technically grounded, and the dynamic volumetric design is thoughtfully engineered for heterogeneous multi-view data. The evaluation is unusually broad, spanning 268 tasks, many policies, and both single- and multi-task settings; results are consistently in favor of SPA. The auxiliary analyses (pose estimation probe, SPA-MAE ablation, real-world tests) strengthen the causal link between 3D awareness and downstream performance. The main concerns are practical and fairness-oriented: reliance on posed multi-view data, limited reporting on efficiency, under-specified tuning for multimodal baselines, and a focus on imitation learning with frozen encoders. These issues, while important, do not undercut the central contribution and the weight of evidence. Overall, I believe SPA is a significant advance for embodied representation learning and is suitable for ICLR; addressing the raised questions (pose robustness, efficiency quantification, stronger baseline tuning notes, and data-efficiency curves) would further enhance the paper.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_065.assessment:
This paper tackles an important question—certified unlearning in a pre-train–fine-tune pipeline—through a theoretically grounded treatment in topic models. The main conceptual contribution, that releasing only the task-adapted predictor B enables provably larger deletion capacity and avoids modifying the base model, is interesting and potentially impactful for how unlearned models are released. The technical approach is sensible given the anchor-word framework and uses standard DP tools to certify indistinguishability. However, the current presentation has notable gaps that limit confidence and perceived impact: (i) the deletion capacity scales poorly with vocabulary size and number of topics, plausibly yielding very small capacities in realistic regimes; (ii) there is no empirical demonstration validating stability of anchors, efficacy versus retraining, or runtime claims; and (iii) several clarity issues (notably the object released in Algorithm 2 and the precise group-privacy calibration) impede full verification. Overall, I view the work as a promising theoretical step with a thought-provoking downstream result, but I recommend revisions that add empirical validation, sharpen technical details, and provide realistic parameter studies to make a strong case for ICLR. My inclination is borderline reject in the present form, with a path to acceptance after addressing the above concerns.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_023.assessment:
This paper presents a timely and practically valuable contribution: a large-scale spatial audio dataset (BEWO-1M), a controllable azimuth-guided diffusion framework (SpatialSonic), and stereo-specific evaluation metrics. The methodological core—explicit azimuth state matrices and regional image perception fused with diffusion—is well-motivated and yields consistent improvements on simulated and small real-world test sets, with both objective and subjective gains. The main caveats are the heavy reliance on simulation without HRTF/head-shadow effects, some clarity gaps in the model specification, and limited real-world evaluation. The inference-time use of GPT for spatial attribute induction also raises reproducibility concerns. Despite these issues, the work opens a new and important line in controllable spatial audio generation, and the dataset/metrics are likely to benefit the community. I lean toward acceptance, conditioned on improved clarity, expanded real-world validation, and stronger ablations around LLM dependence and evaluation robustness.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_077.assessment:
PivotMesh presents a compelling and practical step toward scaling native mesh generation to large, heterogeneous datasets. The combination of a transformer-based hierarchical auto-encoder and a pivot-guided coarse-to-fine generator is conceptually clean and empirically effective, yielding consistent gains over PolyGen and MeshGPT and enabling useful conditional workflows. The work is timely given the community’s interest in compact, renderable geometry, and the paper’s experiments span both small and large-scale regimes.
That said, there are notable gaps: missing baselines (e.g., Polydiff), limited quantitative evidence for compactness and geometric validity, and some ambiguities about baseline training fairness and data filtering. The choices around pivot selection and token ordering raise concerns about invariance and robustness that deserve further analysis. Despite these issues, the core idea is strong and the results are promising; with improved experimental transparency and additional diagnostics on compactness/validity and sensitivity, this work would be a valuable contribution. I lean toward a weak accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_005.assessment:
This is a solid and timely empirical study of long-context behavior that advances the state of practice along two axes: (i) harder, multi-step threading tasks probing position-sensitive retrieval at extreme scales and (ii) a tokenizer-agnostic effective context length metric that the community can adopt for fairer cross-model comparisons. The breadth of models, the scale beyond 100k tokens, and the careful analysis of directionality and position effects offer meaningful insights. However, some methodological choices (UUID-heavy synthetic inputs, LLM-based output parsing, fixed ECL threshold) and uneven coverage at the longest lengths temper the strength of the conclusions. The “thread-safe” claim appears to be context-dependent, weakening in natural text, and would benefit from deeper stress testing. The work would be stronger with more character-based reporting in the main results, sensitivity analyses for ECL, and tighter positioning relative to concurrent chain-reasoning benchmarks like NeedleChain and Sequential-NIAH. Overall, I view this as a valuable benchmark/analysis paper with practical impact and recommend acceptance after addressing the above clarifications and minor revisions.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_041.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and underexplored problem—dataset pruning for instance segmentation—by proposing a simple, training-free, shape-based scoring mechanism with principled normalizations for scale and class imbalance. The method is elegant, computationally trivial, and delivers consistent accuracy gains and dramatic speedups over strong, adapted training-based baselines across COCO, VOC, and Cityscapes, with positive cross-architecture transfer. The main reservations concern limited novelty beyond a well-chosen geometric ratio, sensitivity to annotation quality, and missing comparisons to relevant training-free selection paradigms (e.g., diversity-aware or foundation-embedding-based selection, and OFDS-style object-level selection under budgets). Despite these, the practicality, empirical strength, and clear problem framing make this a valuable contribution that can serve as a strong baseline and spur further research in training-free pruning for structured prediction tasks. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on clarifying methodological details and broadening related-work discussion and, if possible, adding a lightweight training-free baseline comparison.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_035.assessment:
Overall, the paper presents a clear, principled formulation of multi-value alignment with an interpretable user interface (value palettes), a tractable dual for determining composite rewards, and useful theoretical connections to scalarization and sequential updates. The core mathematical machinery is well-known (exponential tilting, KL I-projection, coordinate ascent/IPF), and the paper’s primary value lies in integrating these ideas into a coherent, practical alignment workflow with some theoretical clarifications. Empirically, MAP appears promising, but the evaluation leans heavily on proxy reward models, includes only modest baselines for MORL/DPO, and lacks human studies and more rigorous feasibility diagnostics. The strong claim that linear combinations suffice for the full Pareto frontier requires more careful conditions and related-work context. With strengthened theory exposition (assumptions and limits), clearer connections to classical results, and more robust empirical validation (including human evaluation and stronger baselines), this work could become a solid contribution. As it stands, I view it as a borderline submission leaning toward rejection due to missing theoretical caveats and experimental depth, while acknowledging the practicality and clarity of the proposed framework.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_083.assessment:
This is a timely and compelling paper that tackles a central bottleneck in VLA scaling: dependence on action-labeled robot data. The proposed LAPA framework is conceptually elegant—learning a shared, discrete latent action space from raw videos and using it as pretraining targets—and it delivers strong empirical results across simulation and real robots, including cross-embodiment gains and positive transfer from human videos. The ablations and qualitative analyses further support the core ideas. The main reservations involve the fairness/rigor of some comparisons (particularly the efficiency claim and VPT/IDM setup), limited temporal conditioning in the current design, and sparse details on instruction alignment for pretraining videos. Nonetheless, the contribution is substantial, the method is practical, and the potential impact for scaling robotics foundation models is high. I recommend acceptance after addressing clarity and fairness concerns, adding more controlled efficiency reporting, and, where feasible, including brief diagnostics on codebook utilization and instruction alignment.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_095.assessment:
This paper presents an interesting and conceptually clean idea: learnable spacetimes that jointly encode distances and causality by decoupling a neural quasi-metric for space from a neural partial order for time. The embedding guarantees that relate temporal dimensionality to poset width and that provide low-distortion (and isometric in large D) embeddings are theoretically valuable. However, the current empirical evaluation leaves key questions unanswered. Baselines appear unstable or incomparable, temporal evaluation focuses on edges only (omitting anti-chain preservation), and global versus local distortion is unclear. Some implementation and complexity claims are also inconsistent or underspecified. With strengthened, fair comparisons (including order-embedding and trainable Lorentzian baselines), global distortion and anti-chain metrics, and clearer training constraints, this work could become a substantial contribution. In its current form, I view it as a promising theoretical/methodological advance with underpowered empirical support for ICLR standards, and I would recommend a revision focusing on rigorous, comprehensive experiments and clarified methodology.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_007.assessment:
This paper offers a timely, well-executed empirical study of a practically important phenomenon: non-adversarial reproduction of pretraining data by LLMs under benign prompts. The methodology is simple, interpretable, and conservative; the evaluation is broad, thoughtfully controlled against human baselines, and includes real-world logs. The findings are clear and consequential—average overlap of 7–15%, with a notable long tail; expository/code tasks are particularly affected; prompting reduces averages but not worst cases—directly informing developer practices and policy discussions on attribution, copyright, and model safety. The main limitations stem from the necessary use of a web proxy and a single overlap-length threshold, which blends boilerplate with genuinely rare regurgitation; however, the authors are transparent, and the long-tail/“known-vs-unseen” analyses partly address this. I believe the work will be valuable to the ICLR community and beyond, and recommend acceptance after minor revisions focused on sensitivity analyses, uncertainty reporting, and deeper frequency-aware disambiguation of boilerplate vs memorization.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_001.assessment:
This paper addresses an important, underexplored facet of spatial reasoning in VLMs: the ambiguity of frames of reference and the cross-cultural variability of conventions. The COMFORT protocol is thoughtfully designed, introduces principled and interpretable metrics, and yields insightful findings: default English-like conventions, lack of flexible perspective-taking, and weak robustness/consistency. While some methodological choices (probability reliance, binary QA, machine-translated multilingual prompts, single-model cross-lingual analysis) limit the strength of a few claims, the overall contribution is significant and timely. The work is likely to catalyze better evaluation practices and training strategies for spatially grounded, culturally aware multimodal systems. I recommend acceptance, with encouragement to (i) add forced-choice/contrastive evaluations, (ii) include a small human validation of acceptance regions, and (iii) broaden the multilingual analysis beyond a single model to solidify cross-cultural conclusions.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_025.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely question—understanding and acting on the influence of individual experiences in off-policy RL—and proposes a creative, computationally efficient surrogate to LOO based on per-experience masking and flipped masks. The empirical results convincingly demonstrate substantial runtime advantages and provide preliminary evidence via self-influence signs and selective amendment improvements. However, the central approximation lacks strong validation: there is no direct accuracy comparison to LOO on tractable settings, the method’s practical feasibility (mask storage/overlap, grouping) clouds the “per-experience” claim, and training-time impacts and hyperparameter sensitivities are underexplored. Overall, I find the idea promising and potentially impactful, but the current evidence base is not yet sufficient for a top-tier RL venue. Strengthening the theoretical assumptions or, more pragmatically, providing careful small-scale LOO validations, ablations on mask design, clear reporting on scalability/overheads, and broader, statistically solid comparisons to replay curation baselines would significantly improve the paper. I lean toward rejection in its current form, with encouragement to resubmit after addressing these issues.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_019.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and practical problem: consolidating a large set of heterogeneous, fine-tuned T2I models into one versatile, steerable model. The proposed solution—multi-teacher score distillation into a style-conditioned student augmented with feature imitation and a multi-class adversary—is technically sound, straightforward to implement, and empirically effective by the introduced FIDt measure and qualitative examples. The style codebook affords intuitive control and compositionality, and the distributed training setup is a pragmatic enabler for large models. However, the experimental validation would be stronger with broader baselines (especially training-free merging like Diffusion Soup), more comprehensive quantitative metrics beyond FIDt, and longer-horizon continual learning tests. Some loss definitions need clarification, and a deeper analysis of the general-data distillation assumption would increase confidence. Overall, the work presents a compelling and useful baseline for “merging by distillation” in diffusion T2I, likely to be of practical interest to both researchers and practitioners. With strengthened comparisons and expanded evaluation, it would be well-suited for ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_071.assessment:
This paper presents a fresh and thought-provoking perspective on spatial control in diffusion models by attributing a substantial portion of object placement to localized “trigger patches” in the initial noise. The posterior entropy metric, injection experiments, direct detection from noise, and statistical tests together provide converging (albeit imperfect) evidence for the phenomenon and its utility. The applications—simple reject sampling to remove or align triggers—are practical and show sizeable improvements on custom metrics.
However, several aspects need strengthening for a top-tier venue. The empirical evidence relies heavily on a single object detector for both measurement and supervision, lacks standardized spatial benchmarks and error quantification, and explores only a narrow set of object classes. The “outlier” hypothesis, while intriguing, is not yet deeply characterized; richer analyses (e.g., spectral/statistical decomposition) could both solidify the claim and link it to known architectural biases. Finally, a direct comparison or complementary study with established spatial alignment methods (inference-time and training-based) would considerably sharpen the contribution’s positioning.
Overall, I view the idea as original and potentially impactful, but the paper would benefit from broader evaluations, stronger statistical treatment, and deeper analysis of the underlying mechanism before publication at ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_043.assessment:
This paper delivers a substantial and timely theoretical contribution by establishing the (almost) optimal batch–regret tradeoff for stochastic linear contextual bandits in both context-blind and context-aware regimes, across the full parameter range. The algorithmic design is conceptually simple, yet technically nontrivial, hinging on a clever scaled-and-clipped update and a new matrix concentration inequality for dynamically increasing upper bounds. The results strictly improve prior work in generality and simplicity and appear to be tight thanks to matching lower bounds, including a compelling separation argument for the context-blind setting. The writing is generally clear, though the current draft suffers from some notation/formatting artifacts that should be cleaned up, and a deeper comparison to recent matrix concentration advances would bolster the novelty claim. While empirical results are not essential here, even lightweight experiments would strengthen the “easy to implement” claim. Overall, the paper meets the bar for a top-tier theory contribution and should be of broad interest to the ICLR community working on bandits, online learning with limited adaptivity, and statistical decision-making under resource constraints.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_037.assessment:
This work presents a practical, well-motivated curriculum strategy for diffusion model training, grounded in empirical observations of timestep-wise difficulty and a simple information-theoretic proxy. The approach is easy to implement, broadly applicable, and shows consistent improvements—substantial on DiT/ImageNet—and faster convergence without model changes or extra parameters. While the connection between D_KL monotonicity and learning difficulty remains heuristic, and some baselines and theoretical underpinnings are missing, the empirical breadth, clean design, and orthogonality to established techniques make the contribution valuable. Strengthening the experimental baselines (in particular, dynamic timestep reweighting without staging and comparisons to multi-stage architectures), adding compute-normalized reporting and statistical confidence, and clarifying the KL estimation would elevate the paper further. On balance, I find the paper a solid and practically useful contribution that would interest the ICLR community.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_049.assessment:
This is a strong theoretical paper that meaningfully advances hidden-state privacy analysis for DP-SGD. The Hölder reduction lemma and forward W∞ tracking are novel and technically sound contributions that resolve an open question by removing convexity/smoothness assumptions while still proving convergent RDP. The work also yields strictly tighter bounds in the smooth strongly-convex regime compared to prior state of the art, and it cleanly handles multiple minibatch schemes. The main shortcomings are on the empirical and practical fronts: the accountant is not instantiated as a ready-to-use tool; empirical results are illustrative rather than comprehensive; and some assumptions (bounded domains, global Hölder continuity) may be challenging in practice. Nonetheless, the conceptual advances are clear and significant, and the results will be valuable for both theorists and practitioners seeking tighter DP accounting beyond composition. I recommend acceptance, with a suggestion to bolster the practical aspects (implementation and additional experiments) in the camera-ready.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_011.assessment:
This paper makes a valuable and timely contribution by redefining test-time poisoning under realistic constraints and by introducing a practical attack framework (surrogate distillation + in-distribution regularization + TTA-aware objectives). The empirical study is broad and convincingly shows that prior risk assessments can be overstated under white-box/offline assumptions, while also demonstrating that the proposed attacks remain potent against several TTA methods. The work offers actionable insight into simple defenses, which is useful to practitioners.
The novelty is moderate (many components build on known ideas—surrogate models, feature-distribution matching, entropy-based objectives), but their integration for grey-box, online TTDP and the careful protocol design are meaningful and fill an evaluation gap. The main limitations are under-specification of some attack parameters, limited sensitivity analyses (poison ratio, budget), and a defense study focused on a single baseline. The theoretical argument for in-distribution transfer is intuitive but light.
Overall, I lean toward acceptance. The paper will help calibrate discussions of TTA robustness, provide a more realistic benchmark for future methods, and offer practical guidance on both attacking and defending TTA in deployment-like settings.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_067.assessment:
The paper tackles an important and timely problem and proposes a neat, easily implemented training-time mechanism that yields promising initial attribution accuracy with limited performance cost. However, serious concerns remain about security (spoofing/forgery), robustness to realistic transformations, evaluation biases, and missing comparisons to closely related provenance-tagging work (notably DEBUGLM) and stronger attribution baselines. The single-source assumption and reliance on imperceptible Unicode characters further constrain practical applicability: invisible codepoints are easy to remove or insert, and regeneration-based defenses presuppose audit-time access to the trained model.
Overall, I view the contribution as an interesting prototype demonstrating that LLMs can learn to emit provider-linked tags with minimal architectural changes. To reach ICLR standards, the work needs (i) a principled, attack-resilient attribution protocol (e.g., keyed detection, anti-spoofing, content-binding), (ii) stronger, unbiased evaluations under realistic conditions and stronger baselines, and (iii) a direct comparison to training-time provenance approaches such as DEBUGLM. I recommend rejection at this time, with encouragement to revise along these lines.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_055.assessment:
This paper makes a compelling and practical case for hyperbolic prototypes in OOD detection, showing that simply replacing the Euclidean classifier with balanced hyperbolic prototypes and a distance-based cross-entropy often boosts a wide variety of scorers, while also enabling hierarchical OOD evaluation. The idea is intuitive, the empirical coverage is broad, and the proposed balancing addresses a real shortcoming of off-the-shelf hyperbolic embeddings on imbalanced trees. However, there are important clarity issues in the loss definitions, some overstated claims (a few scorers/datasets regress), and fairness concerns regarding temperature settings and hyperparameter tuning. The experimental baseline set could be strengthened with Euclidean prototype/training controls and additional contemporary methods (GradPCA, GROOD, BBAS, Catalyst). Overall, I view the contribution as moderately novel but useful to the community: it provides a simple, geometry-aware training recipe that improves OOD without bespoke scorers, and the hierarchical OOD evaluation is a nice addition. With clarifications and minor experimental tightening, I would support acceptance as a solid, practical paper that highlights the role of geometry in OOD detection.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_089.assessment:
This paper puts forward a simple and elegant modification of heavy-ball—motivated by a 2×2 ODE with a strong Lyapunov function and a carefully chosen over-relaxed gradient—that achieves global accelerated convergence for smooth strongly convex problems, and extends naturally to composite and bilinear saddle-point settings. The theoretical development is sound and unifies several strands of accelerated method design; the algorithms are single-loop with few tunable parameters, and the empirical results are consistent and competitive. While the rate constants are not the sharpest known, and practical issues such as parameter dependence and broader empirical validation remain, the contribution is conceptually clear, technically meaningful, and of definite interest to the ICLR community. I recommend acceptance, with suggestions to sharpen comparisons and expand robustness experiments.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_073.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and understudied problem: the adversarial robustness of data attribution used for compensation. The threat model is well-motivated, and the two attack families are complementary and practical. Empirical results are strong across tasks, models, and attribution methods, and the stability theorem provides helpful intuition for why black-box manipulations persist after retraining. The primary weaknesses lie in missing practical constraints (detection/realism, utility impact, and query costs), some ambiguity about the compensation rule with respect to signed influence, and limited baselines/sensitivity analyses. Despite these gaps, the work is novel, timely, and likely to stimulate substantial follow-up on both attacks and defenses in data valuation pipelines. With clarifications and additional analyses addressing the questions above, this would be a strong contribution. I lean toward acceptance given significance and novelty, while encouraging the authors to expand empirical robustness, baselines, and discussion of mitigations.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_079.assessment:
This paper delivers a timely and comprehensive empirical study of model merging with a deliberate focus on compositional generalization, which is closer to real-world motivations than standard multitask retention. The breadth across modalities, inclusion of key baselines, and careful analysis of prerequisites, computational costs, hyperparameter sensitivity, and scaling make this a valuable resource for the community. While the study is limited to single backbones per modality and omits some relevant method families in experiments, the authors acknowledge these gaps and provide an extensible framework and code. The findings—especially modality-dependent trends, the interference–coverage trade-off with scaling, and practical guidance on method choice—are likely to influence both future research and practice. I recommend acceptance. Strengthening the generation metrics, adding wall-clock/memory reporting, clarifying LoRA-specific merging details, and expanding the NLP analysis would further enhance the paper.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_085.assessment:
The paper tackles an important and practical problem—training-free NAS for lightweight NLU models—and proposes a refreshingly simple proxy that is easy to compute and, in the presented search space, correlates well with downstream quality. The empirical results show the approach can select strong architectures with negligible search cost and achieve competitive GLUE/SQuAD performance after standard pretraining and distillation. However, the central novelty is modest: PCA_dim at initialization is tightly coupled with width, and multiplying by #params risks largely re-encoding model size. The paper would be significantly strengthened by: (i) fair, head-to-head comparisons with recent Transformer-specific, training-free proxies (notably ZeroLM) that already report stronger FlexiBERT correlations; (ii) analyses that disentangle PCA_dim’s contribution from #params (e.g., partial correlations, fixed-budget studies); and (iii) clarifying the exact data/procedure used to compute the proxy in ranking experiments. As it stands, I view this as a promising and useful baseline with practical value, but with overstatements (e.g., “first” to apply zero-shot NAS to NLU) and important missing comparisons. I lean toward a weak reject pending these additions; with the suggested revisions and broader comparisons, it could be a solid contribution for ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_091.assessment:
This is a timely and compelling paper that brings diffusion score-matching into IRL in a principled way. The proposed DS divergence and the practical variance-corrected estimator yield a simple, stable, and regression-only training loop that slots neatly into standard RL policy optimization. Theoretical results are strong and, to my knowledge, novel in the IRL context: instance-dependent small-loss/second-order bounds with linear-in-H dependence on estimation/optimization errors and a clean path to avoiding compounding errors. Empirically, results on state-only IL with humanoids are impressive and substantiate the stability and effectiveness claims against representative baselines.
The primary shortcoming is the limited empirical comparison set in the state-only regime—several recent non-adversarial or primal LfO methods are missing, and more ablations on design choices would help. There are also a few clarity issues in the theorem statement/notation and strong realizability assumptions that would benefit from discussion and, ideally, robustness analyses. Despite these, I view the contribution as significant: it offers a new, practically attractive direction for IRL that is likely to influence follow-up work. I recommend acceptance, contingent on stronger comparisons and additional clarifications.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_031.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and under-explored problem: policy-based RLHF without reward inference in general stochastic MDPs. The core idea—using human preferences to form local value-difference estimates and drive a zeroth-order policy gradient—is elegant and meaningfully different from DPO-style methods. The theoretical analysis is careful and contributes new insights on balancing inverse-link bias and trajectory variance. However, the current empirical validation is limited (small tabular domain, incomplete quantitative reporting), the human query model is impractical (large M per pair), and key robustness aspects (unknown/misspecified links, annotator heterogeneity) are not addressed. There is also a presentation inconsistency in ZBCPG’s scaling. Overall, I find the theoretical contribution promising and novel, but I recommend a borderline/weak reject at this stage due to experimental and practicality gaps. With stronger empirical evidence (including high-dimensional function approximation), robustness analyses, corrected algorithmic details, and more realistic query models, this work could make a valuable contribution to ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_013.assessment:
This paper delivers a useful and timely contribution: a depth-aware differentiable curve rasterizer paired with perspective-correct projection and SDS-based supervision to enable text-to-3D and single-image-to-3D sketch generation. The approach addresses concrete limitations in prior differentiable curve renderers (notably 3Doodle’s projection and occlusion handling) and produces compelling qualitative results, with quantitative metrics and a user study that, while not definitive, are supportive. The technical core is sound and practically relevant, though certain aspects (gradient behavior at occlusion changes, scalability of closest-point solving, and heuristic denoising) deserve deeper analysis. Some experimental comparisons are not entirely fair to reconstruction methods, and related work around recent Gaussian-splatting-based differentiable curve pipelines could be better contextualized. Overall, I view this as an original, valuable step toward 3D sketch generation with a reusable rendering component. With clarifications and strengthened evaluations as suggested, I recommend acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_097.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem: the stability and uncertainty of LLM evaluators. The empirical sweep across models and prompts provides useful practical insights, and the idea of augmenting evaluators with response confidence is interesting. However, the current evidence base has notable methodological gaps. The reliance on raw token probabilities without calibration undermines cross-model conclusions; “evaluation confidence” is not consistently tied to correctness; CoT effects may be confounded by prompt design; and the OOD study, while valuable, is small and lacks critical baselines (e.g., higher-confidence-wins heuristic) and fairness controls regarding who sees u1/u2. As a result, the central claims—especially about mitigating and utilizing uncertainty—are only partially substantiated. I encourage the authors to strengthen calibration analyses, add simple and fair baselines, expand OOD coverage, and more rigorously connect confidence to accuracy. With these improvements, the work could make a strong contribution to the ICLR community. In its current form, I lean toward rejection, albeit with recognition of the paper’s potential and the practical relevance of the insights.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_003.assessment:
This paper presents a careful and thought-provoking empirical study showing that distributed representations, when trained with next-step prediction on multi-object videos, can develop linearly separable object codes and match or exceed slot-based models in dynamics prediction while approaching them in reconstruction quality. The proposed separability probe is simple and broadly applicable, and the analysis of partially entangled yet separable codes is insightful. However, the work is primarily empirical with moderate methodological novelty, and several evaluation aspects need strengthening and clarification: the dynamics accuracy metric is nonstandard and under-specified; compute/capacity parity is unclear; comparisons omit several strong recent baselines; and there is no direct OOD compositional generalization assessment. Overall, I find the central message important and the results promising, but I would like to see clearer evaluation definitions, stronger baselines, and tighter controls to merit publication at ICLR. I lean toward a borderline accept if the authors can clarify the methodology and augment experiments as suggested; otherwise, this remains a solid and timely empirical contribution that could be strengthened for a top-tier venue.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_009.assessment:
This paper presents a well-motivated and practically useful combination of variational transport (neural JKO via CNFs) with a simple, principled importance-based rejection/resampling that provides both i.i.d. sampling and tractable density evaluation, together with a monotone KL-decrease guarantee for the rejection map. The theoretical linkage to Wasserstein gradient flows is solid for exact JKO steps, and the empirical results on multimodal targets are promising. The main shortcomings are (i) a theory–practice gap: convergence is proved for exact JKO steps but not for their neural approximations; (ii) overstatements about avoiding the curse of dimensionality; and (iii) incomplete experimental context (missing some relevant baselines, limited sensitivity analyses, and some presentation issues in tables). Overall, I find the contribution novel and significant enough for ICLR, contingent on clarifying the independence claim, tightening the theoretical narrative regarding learned approximations, and strengthening/cleaning the empirical section. I lean toward acceptance after revisions.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_029.assessment:
This paper provides a compelling, principled account of random-walk-based graph learning, offering clean probabilistic invariance conditions, universality-in-probability guarantees linked to cover times, and actionable insights on information propagation. The “anonymization + named neighbors” design and the adoption of MDLR/non-backtracking/restarters are well-justified and borne out empirically. The experimental demonstrations, especially perfect SR25 separation and competitive training-free ogbn-arxiv performance with LLMs, underline the practical promise. The main reservations concern scalability and fairness (dependence on large LMs, limited compute-normalized comparisons), missing ablations tying theory to accuracy, and limited discussion of generalization in isomorphism experiments. Overall, I view this as a strong contribution that advances understanding and practice of RWNNs; with additional ablations and clarifications, it would be a valuable addition to ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_045.assessment:
This paper presents a thought-provoking and well-articulated proof-of-concept that a frozen LLM, with minimal adapters and an unsupervised reconstruction-capable visual tokenizer, can process and autoregressively generate low-level visual features without multimodal supervision. The methodology is clean, the ablations are targeted and informative, and the insights about the importance of the visual module and AR generation are valuable. However, the empirical evaluation is limited: baselines are weak for restoration, datasets and metrics do not follow standard low-level vision practice, and no real-world benchmarks or efficiency analyses are provided. While the contribution is conceptually interesting and may spark follow-up work on minimal-parameter multimodal adaptation and visual tokenization for pixel-faithful control, the current experimental evidence falls short of ICLR’s bar for comprehensive validation. Strengthening the evaluation with standard datasets and competitive baselines, clarifying multi-task training and task-token capacity, and reporting efficiency would substantially improve the paper’s case. As it stands, I view the work as a solid exploratory study with promising ideas but insufficient empirical rigor for acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_039.assessment:
The paper addresses an important and active topic—how to encode and fuse graphs with LLMs under token and compute constraints—and reports consistent improvements over a strong recent baseline (G-Retriever). The two components, LGPT and Early Query Fusion, are simple, modular, and appear effective in practice. However, the methodological novelty is moderate: LGPT is closely related to established latent-token/multi-token pooling approaches in both the set/graph and multimodal literature (Set Transformer, Perceiver(-IO), Graph Multiset Transformer, DGTL-like injections), and Early Query Fusion follows a well-trodden virtual-node paradigm (QA-GNN lineage). The experimental study lacks key comparisons to alternative multi-token pooling baselines, has limited statistical reporting, and omits concrete efficiency measurements—important given the work’s scalability motivation. Clarity would also benefit from a more precise architectural specification and a fuller related-work discussion.
Overall, I find the idea promising and the empirical gains encouraging, but the paper in its current form falls short of ICLR’s bar for originality and rigor. Strengthening baselines (especially attention-based multi-token readouts), adding variance/efficiency analyses, and better situating LGPT within the latent-token literature would substantially improve the submission. I lean toward a borderline rejection, with a clear path to a stronger resubmission.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_061.assessment:
COMETH makes a substantive and well-executed contribution: it is, to my knowledge, the first continuous-time discrete-state diffusion model specialized for graphs with principled rate design and prior matching, and it replaces a complex structural feature suite with a single, well-justified random-walk encoding. The theoretical clarifications (Proposition 1 and RRWP capabilities/limitations) add rigor. Empirically, the model is strong: it achieves excellent VUN on synthetic graphs, competitive to superior results on QM9, and notably higher validity/VUN on large-scale molecular datasets; conditional generation improvements are also compelling. The main areas for improvement are a more careful analysis of τ-leaping approximations, fuller efficiency comparisons to discrete-time counterparts, sensitivity analyses for MMD, and positioning relative to recent spectral/latent generative approaches. Despite these, the paper is technically solid, clear, and practically impactful. I recommend acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_063.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem—overrefusal in already-safe LLMs—and offers a practical, data-centric solution: overgenerating with stronger teachers to shift the safety–usefulness frontier favorably, followed by a targeted preference optimization procedure (POROver) to further reduce overrefusal while largely preserving safety. The empirical gains, especially on OR-Bench, are compelling, and the breadth of evaluations and replications across student families bolster the case for generality. However, the evaluation stack leans heavily on proprietary judges and a single safety classifier used both for selection and for evaluation, creating potential circularity and bias; human evaluations are limited. The reliance on GPT-4o also raises accessibility concerns, though the Llama-3-70B ablation helps. I view this as a strong, practice-relevant contribution with solid engineering and clear insights, but I would like to see strengthened evaluator diversity and human assessments, plus ablations on POROver design, to fully warrant a top-tier bar. Overall, I lean positive: the problem is important, the approach is sensible and effective, and the community would benefit from the datasets and methodology, especially if the authors can address the evaluation and ablation gaps.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_057.assessment:
This work tackles an important and underexplored problem: developing general, comparable diagnostics for how RL agents explore across diverse algorithms and tasks. Modeling learning as a trajectory in occupancy-measure space and quantifying its efficiency (ESL) and productive directionality (OMR) via optimal transport is a conceptually strong and potentially impactful idea. The empirical narrative—showing these metrics can illuminate differences among algorithms and capture task hardness—is promising. However, key technical and practical aspects require clearer exposition and deeper validation, including precise metric definitions, sensitivity to the choice of ground cost and checkpoint frequency, the strength of the connection between OMR and regret, and computational scalability. With strengthened theoretical clarifications and additional ablations, this paper could provide a valuable, broadly applicable tool for the RL community. As it stands, I lean toward a positive evaluation based on originality and potential impact, tempered by requests for clarifications and further experiments to solidify the claims.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_081.assessment:
This paper presents a simple yet powerful inference-time jailbreak that composes a strong aligned model with small safe/unsafe models via a ratio-of-experts scheme, producing near-saturated ASR on standard harmful-instruction benchmarks. The early-token divergence analysis neatly motivates the design, and the empirical breadth across families, scales, and languages is compelling. However, the methodological novelty over prior decoding-time composition methods (DExperts/contrastive decoding/proxy-tuning) is limited, and some claims (compute, diminishing influence) would benefit from tighter evidence. The experimental section would be substantially strengthened by direct comparisons to the closest baselines, fuller reporting of human evaluation, and a clearer compute accounting. The proposed defense is preliminary relative to contemporary system-2 or representation-based defenses. Overall, I find the work impactful as a risk demonstration with a clean, reproducible attack that many will care about; with revisions addressing the missing baselines and clarifying claims, it would make a strong contribution. My recommendation is a weak accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_015.assessment:
This paper presents a timely and carefully designed benchmark for evaluating explanation utility through simulatability under distribution shift, a concrete and necessary criterion related to faithfulness and completeness. The experimental suite is broad, the methodology is principled, and the validation via both synthetic models and human predictors is thoughtful. The central negative result—no tested method reliably beats an explanation-free control—carries important implications for the field. The main caveats are the reliance on GPT-4 as the predictor, relatively limited explanation instantiations (especially for saliency and counterfactuals), and a constrained set of models-under-explanation. Even with these limitations, ALMANACS addresses a real need, is well executed, and is likely to be of significant value to the community. I recommend acceptance, with encouragement to expand predictors, explanation formats, and ablations in future versions.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_027.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely challenge—scaling RAG for long-context summarization—by introducing a simple and appealing idea: use LLM-generated historical responses to connect document chunks in a graph and learn retrieval-aware node embeddings via a self-supervised ranking objective. The method is conceptually clean, easy to integrate, and achieves consistent, if modest, gains over classic retrievers across four datasets; ablations support the contribution of each component. However, the empirical evaluation lacks several closely related and contemporary baselines (GraphRAG, LightPAL, MemoRAG, strong long-context LLMs), and the test sets are small without significance analysis, limiting confidence. Key design details (graph construction specifics, supervision signal beyond the trivial positive) and the true cost of indexing new documents (which includes non-negligible LLM calls) are insufficiently addressed. Overall, I view GoR as a promising direction with a neat formulation, but the current evaluation and analysis are not yet at the level expected for ICLR. I recommend rejection at this time, with a clear path to strengthening: add stronger baselines and fair long-context comparisons, provide comprehensive cost accounting, clarify graph design, and deepen analyses of when/why response nodes help and how to safeguard against hallucination propagation.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_033.assessment:
Autoverse is a promising idea: an evolvable, CA-inspired, GPU-accelerated game DSL that could serve as a fertile ground for open-ended learning and curriculum emergence. The integration of search-based environment evolution, imitation-from-search, and a regret-based UED loop is conceptually appealing. However, the current experimental evidence is too preliminary for a top-tier venue. The paper lacks rigorous quantitative evaluation of the end-to-end pipeline, ablations and comparisons to strong baselines (e.g., PAIRED/REPAIRED, PPO from scratch, Procgen/Avalon-style evaluations), and does not substantiate the claimed acceleration with concrete throughput metrics. Additionally, several technical details—particularly around rule application semantics, search methodology, and environment evolution—need clearer specification. With a more thorough empirical study, stronger baselines, clarified algorithms, and released code, this work could provide substantial value to the community. As it stands, I recommend rejection, with encouragement to resubmit after significant strengthening of the evaluation and clarity.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_021.assessment:
This paper introduces a neat, intuitive, and extremely lightweight modification to momentum that leverages gradient–momentum alignment to downweight misaligned gradients. The idea is plausible and well-motivated, and the authors provide a broad empirical tour showing small but fairly consistent gains over SGDM on standard image tasks, mixed but often positive improvements over AdamW in LLM fine-tuning, and clearer benefits in online learning and as a warm-up phase. The paper is clearly written and the method is easy to adopt, with a practical learning-rate transfer rule.
However, the novelty is moderate, the theoretical development is largely heuristic, and the main classification gains are small (often within what could be run-to-run variance). More importantly, the paper does not empirically compare to several closely related momentum/damping adaptations (adaptive heavy-ball without manual tuning, kinetic/cubic damping, Dyna-like damping control) that are natural baselines given the paper’s damping rationale. The LLM evaluation would benefit from reporting absolute scores/variances and from comparisons against stronger modern baselines tailored to LLMs; the online learning results should be contextualized with plasticity-preserving methods. These gaps make it difficult to fully assess the significance of TAM relative to the current state of the art in stabilizing momentum dynamics.
Overall, I view TAM as a promising and practical tweak with encouraging preliminary breadth, but the empirical evidence is not yet strong enough—relative to missing baselines and small margins—to warrant publication at ICLR. Strengthening comparisons, expanding statistical analyses, and clarifying the LLM evaluation would substantially improve the case.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_087.assessment:
This paper takes a fresh and pragmatic angle on FL backdoor defense by introducing a malignity-aware feature (neuron-level BE via Lipschitz surrogates) and a distributionally robust clustering scheme (Wasserstein K-Means on concentrated BE). The approach is simple, data-free, and achieves strong results against challenging attacks, including 3DFed and a crafted adaptive variant, across several datasets and baselines. The main reservations lie in the limited theoretical tightness and specificity of the BE proxy, the underspecified details and possible overhead of Wasserstein clustering, the reliance on a heuristic cluster-selection rule that can be subverted (with a fallback that reintroduces a majority assumption), and the need for broader stress testing (stronger non-IID, alternative adaptive strategies) to substantiate claims of robustness. Nevertheless, the paper addresses an important problem with a compelling, practical idea and promising empirical results. With clarifications and stronger ablations, it would be a valuable contribution. I lean toward a weak accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_099.assessment:
This paper presents a practically meaningful and well-executed idea: combine the efficiency of memory-based temporal encoders with explicit, multi-hop common-neighbor–based pairwise representations via a scalable GPU implementation. The method is conceptually clean, empirically strong on large-scale TGB (SOTA on Review, competitive elsewhere), and substantially faster than subgraph-based competitors, though slower than pure memory-only baselines. Theoretical analyses support the design intuitions, albeit with some overbroad expressivity claims that would benefit from tighter framing and clearer assumptions. The main areas for improvement are (i) harmonizing evaluation settings and deepening comparisons with strong recent baselines (e.g., TPNet), (ii) providing finer-grained efficiency/memory profiling and sensitivity to K/hop ranges, and (iii) evaluating on more sequence-sensitive regimes to delineate applicability. Overall, I view TNCN as a valuable contribution for scalable temporal link prediction with pairwise inductive bias and recommend acceptance after addressing the above clarifications.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_093.assessment:
Linguini addresses an important and timely gap in the evaluation of large language models by probing language-agnostic linguistic reasoning in real, low-resource languages without assuming prior language knowledge. The benchmark is well-motivated, carefully curated from IOL problems, and the main findings—low absolute performance and a substantive gap between proprietary and open models—are both credible and impactful. The ablations are directionally supportive of the core claims, particularly the heavy reliance on context over memorized knowledge. However, some methodological details and evaluations could be strengthened: scoring normalization and multiple references to reduce false negatives, broader and more systematic transliteration experiments across models, fuller reproducibility details (prompting and decoding), and more extensive per-category analyses. Despite these limitations, the paper’s contribution is significant and likely to be valuable for the ICLR community. With clarifications and moderate extensions, this is a strong benchmark paper that I would be inclined to accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_051.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely question: whether LVLM success on diagram reasoning reflects genuine relational understanding or reliance on background knowledge. The authors present a well-motivated test suite that decomposes skills, pairings of synthetic and real diagrams, and multiple analyses (including knowledge-grounded interventions and complexity-stratified results) that consistently indicate strong entity handling but weak relational understanding. The knowledge-shortcut explanation is persuasive, though not decisively causal; expanding ablations (text-only/image-only, systematic edge flips) and diversifying reasoning tasks beyond counting would further solidify the claims. The work complements recent chart/diagram benchmarks and helps recalibrate community expectations, offering a practical diagnostic for future LVLM development. I view this as a valuable, primarily empirical contribution with moderate originality but high utility. With additional controls and expanded relational tasks, it would be a strong fit; as is, I lean toward a weak accept/borderline accept given its careful study design, breadth of evaluation, and relevance to ICLR’s audience.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_075.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling and novel direction: using internal activations as a high-bandwidth, compute-efficient communication medium between language model agents. The approach is simple, training-light, and, if reliable, could reshape how multi-agent LM systems are designed. The empirical results are promising, including cross-family demonstrations without a shared tokenizer. However, the current evaluation falls short of top-tier standards: datasets are small; core baselines (debate variants, self-consistency, verifier-guided inference) are underexplored; the Biographies evaluation risks judge bias; compute claims rely mostly on analysis rather than rigorous end-to-end measurements; and the optional linear mapping exhibits instability not yet diagnosed. Moreover, qualitative examples include prominent AC errors, underscoring the need for deeper analysis and more robust benchmarking. Overall, I view the idea as original and important, but the evidence is not yet strong enough for ICLR acceptance. With stronger, larger-scale, and fairer comparisons (including compute-normalized baselines), improved methodological diagnostics (layer choice, normalization, mapping), and unbiased evaluation protocols, this line of work could have substantial impact.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_069.assessment:
LJ-Bench makes a valuable and timely contribution: a legally grounded ontology and benchmark that broadens the scope of jailbreak evaluation to 76 crime types, many of which are underrepresented in existing datasets. The ontology/knowledge-graph foundation and the cross-model/attack evaluation provide useful insights, notably that iterative jailbreaks remain effective and that societal/animal harms are often overlooked yet vulnerable. However, several methodological issues temper the strength of the claims: reliance on LLM judges without human calibration, partial use of iterative attacks (mostly for proprietary models), heterogeneous safety settings across targets, and limited statistical treatment (no CIs, no ASR). The paper would be significantly strengthened by adding a human-labeled subset, reporting judge agreement, normalizing or clearly stratifying safety configurations, and supplementing harmfulness scores with ASR and confidence intervals. With these refinements, LJ-Bench could become a go-to artifact for the community. As it stands, I view this as a strong benchmark paper with meaningful contributions and practical value, but recommend conditional acceptance contingent on addressing the key evaluation concerns.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review
